name: LineFORM
short_description: An actuated curve interface built from a serpentine chain of servo motors that can change its own shape for display, interaction, and physical constraint.
long_description: LineFORM is a shape-changing interface from the MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group that explores the design space of actuated lines and curves. Inspired by serpentine robotics, it is a series chain of one-degree-of-freedom servo motors with integrated sensors, letting it dynamically reshape itself while users directly deform, touch, and feel it through variable-stiffness joints. As physical AI / interaction design it matters because it treats a single line as a programmable physical medium for data representation, ambient display, body constraints, and haptic feedback, expanding tangible and shape-changing UI vocabulary beyond flat pin-array displays.
ai_note: It is a mechanical shape-changing actuation system (servo-motor chain with sensors and variable-stiffness joints); no machine learning or AI runs in it.

images.caption: LineFORM, an actuated curve interface composed of a chain of servo motors that reshapes itself for display, interaction, and constraint.
images.alt_text: A long articulated line-like device made of a chain of small motorized segments, bent into a curving shape on a surface.

sources.title: LineFORM
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab, Tangible Media Group

sources.title: LineFORM: Actuated Curve Interfaces for Display, Interaction, and Constraint
LineFORM: Actuated Curve Interfaces for Display, Interaction, and Constraint

interactions.title: A phone that reshapes to its task
interactions.description: The same line of motors curls into a wristband on your arm, straightens into a handset when a call comes in, and lies flat as a tablet-like surface to read on — one object physically reconfiguring itself for each use.

interactions.title: Bend it and it remembers the move
interactions.description: You grab the chain and physically pose it into a curve; the integrated joint sensors record the gesture so the line can replay or hold that shape on its own — programming by direct physical demonstration rather than code.

interactions.title: Joints that stiffen to snap into place
interactions.description: As you push and deform the line, its variable-stiffness joints resist or give way to guide your hand into discrete positions — a physical snap-to-grid you feel in your fingers instead of see on a screen.

interactions.title: A cord that ties and re-routes itself
interactions.description: Demonstrated as shape-changing cords and mobiles, the actuated line coils, wraps, and re-routes on its own — a power cord that tidies itself or a hanging mobile that rearranges its form over time.

interactions.title: A line that becomes a physical constraint
interactions.description: Treating the curve as a boundary or borderline, LineFORM forms a body constraint — wrapping or bracing against a limb to gently limit or guide movement, turning a line into a tangible barrier.

interactions.title: Data you can read off a curve
interactions.description: Because a line can encode abstract data in its shape, LineFORM bends into graphs, gauges, or ambient indicators that physically rise and fall as the underlying values change.

interactions.title: A desk ruler that bends to your measurement
interactions.description: Grounded in its programming-by-demonstration and shape-holding, the line could act as a tool that curves to a target length or angle on command, then stiffens its joints so you can trace or measure against the physical reference.

interactions.title: A posture reminder on your wrist
interactions.description: Building on the wristband form and variable-stiffness joints, the worn line could subtly tighten or shift shape as a tactile nudge — a slow physical cue to sit up or take a break, felt rather than heard.
